attributes.stem: A 43-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-month history of difficulty seeing at night and a sensation of dry eyes. She follows a strict vegan diet, has coeliac disease and usually sees her naturopath for her health. She works as a gardener and states she does not use sunglasses or sun protection, preferring to be closer to nature. She does not take any regular medications except for some herbal teas her naturopath recommends. On examination, she appears thin with a BMI of 17.5 kg/m2. Her vital signs are within normal limits. Ocular examination reveals bilateral grey-white, foamy triangular patches on the temporal conjunctiva. The corneas appear dull and slightly hazy.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic features of vitamin A deficiency manifesting as xerophthalmia. The combination of night blindness (nyctalopia), dry eyes, and most importantly, Bitot spots (the grey-white foamy patches on the conjunctiva) is pathognomonic. The cornea is hazy from corneal xerosis. Risk factors include her restricted vegan diet and malabsorption from coeliac disease. Vitamin A is required to create the photosensitive visual pigments of rods (rhodopsin) which then leads to a person not being able to see in dim light.

Cataracts can cause progressive vision loss but typically affect both day and night vision equally. While they can cause glare and haloes around lights at night, they would not explain the Bitot spots or dry eyes seen in this patient.

Retinitis pigmentosa does cause night blindness as an early symptom due to rod photoreceptor degeneration. However, it is a genetic condition that typically presents in childhood or early adulthood thus not fitting with this patient, and would not be associated with Bitot spots or the nutritional deficiency picture seen here.

Pterygium is a wedge-shaped fibrovascular growth from the conjunctiva onto the cornea, usually on the nasal side. While it can appear triangular and associated with high sun exposure without protection, it does not have the foamy appearance of Bitot spots and would not cause night blindness.

Keratoconjunctivitis sicca (dry eye syndrome) can cause symptoms of ocular dryness but would not explain the night blindness or the presence of Bitot spots. While it can occur in autoimmune conditions associated with coeliac disease, the presentation here is more consistent with vitamin A deficiency.

Treatment of xerophthalmia involves immediate vitamin A supplementation, both to treat the ocular manifestations and prevent progression to more severe forms that can lead to permanent blindness. The underlying cause (in this case, dietary deficiency) should also be addressed.
learningPoints: Xerophthalmia is a clinical manifestation of vitamin A deficiency, presenting with night blindness and conjunctival changes.
Risk factors for vitamin A deficiency include malnutrition (including by restricted diets), malabsorption disorders and intestinal infections.
Bitot spots (grey-white foamy patches on temporal conjunctiva) are pathognomonic for vitamin A deficiency.
references: Xerophthalmia and night blindness for the assessment of clinical vitamin A deficiency in individuals and populations. Vitamin and Mineral Nutrition Information System. Geneva: World Health Organization; 2014.  Available at:  http://apps. who.int/iris/bitstream/10665/133705/1/WHO_NMH_NHD_ EPG_14.4_eng.pdf?ua=1,

Belete GT, Fenta AL, Hussen MS. Xerophthalmia and Its Associated Factors among School-Age Children in Amba Giorgis Town, Northwest Ethiopia, 2018.  Available: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6893271/
